edges.description: Structural lesion burden and complications contribute to bone pain.
Weak dysplastic bone predisposes to fractures.
Lesions in weight-bearing long bones can deform under mechanical stress.
Skeletal pain and deformity cause antalgic gait.
intermediate_mechanisms: Pain, deformity, fracture, coxa vara, and leg-length discrepancy alter gait mechanics.

edges.description: FGF23-mediated renal phosphate wasting lowers circulating phosphate.
Childhood phosphate wasting can produce rickets.
Phosphate wasting can produce osteomalacia in later presentations.
Skeletal and mineral-metabolism complications can contribute to short stature.
intermediate_mechanisms: chronic skeletal disease burden and phosphate wasting can impair growth

nodes.description: Denosumab targets RANKL-driven osteoclastogenesis and has shown promise in case reports, but safety concerns including rebound hypercalcemia mean use should be limited to research protocols.

evidence.reference_title: Fibrous Dysplasia/McCune-Albright Syndrome: Clinical and Translational Perspectives.
evidence.snippet: denosumab may hold promise as a potential treatment for FD, however given these safety concerns its use should be limited to research protocols.

nodes.description: When post-zygotic activating GNAS variants involve extraskeletal tissues, constitutive Gs-alpha/cAMP signaling produces the McCune-Albright spectrum of hyperpigmented skin lesions, hyperfunctioning endocrinopathies, and a small increased risk of neoplasia in affected tissues.

biological_processes: adenylate cyclase-activating G protein-coupled receptor signaling pathway

evidence.reference_title: Fibrous Dysplasia/McCune-Albright Syndrome: Clinical and Translational Perspectives.
evidence.snippet: Constitutive receptor activation results in overproduction of hormones, manifesting clinically as hyperfunctioning endocrinopathies and increased skin pigmentation.

evidence.reference_title: Fibrous Dysplasia/McCune-Albright Syndrome: Clinical and Translational Perspectives.
evidence.snippet: Activating GNAS mutations in FD/MAS are considered weak oncogenes, and may infer a small increased risk of malignant transformation in affected tissues.

nodes.description: Active dysplastic osteogenic cells can overproduce FGF23. FGF23 decreases renal phosphate reabsorption and active vitamin D, causing hypophosphatemia with rickets or osteomalacia in patients with high disease burden.

evidence.reference_title: Fibrous Dysplasia of Bone and McCune-Albright Syndrome: A Bench to Bedside Review.
evidence.snippet: Nearly 50% of patients with FD/MAS exhibit some degree of renal phosphate wasting

evidence.reference_title: Fibrous Dysplasia/McCune-Albright Syndrome: Clinical and Translational Perspectives.
evidence.snippet: Fibroblast growth factor-23 (FGF23) is overproduced by FD cells

evidence.reference_title: Fibrous Dysplasia of Bone and McCune-Albright Syndrome: A Bench to Bedside Review.
evidence.snippet: overt hypophosphatemia is uncommon and typically affects patients with greater overall disease burden

nodes.description: GNAS-activated osteogenic progenitors fail to mature normally. Normal marrow and trabecular bone are replaced by fibro-osseous tissue containing fibrous stroma and immature woven bone, weakening the affected skeleton.
nodes.id: Impaired osteogenic differentiation and marrow replacement

evidence.reference_title: Fibrous Dysplasia/McCune-Albright Syndrome: Clinical and Translational Perspectives.
evidence.snippet: Bone and bone marrow are thus replaced by proliferating BMSCs, resulting in fibro-osseous tissue typically devoid of hematopoietic marrow

evidence.reference_title: Fibrous Dysplasia of Bone and McCune-Albright Syndrome: A Bench to Bedside Review.
evidence.snippet: The immature osteoprogenitors proliferate and produce excess amounts of abnormal bone matrix, consisting predominantly of woven bone.

nodes.description: Intravenous bisphosphonates may be considered for persistent moderate to severe FD-related bone pain after evaluation for fracture, metabolic, or orthopedic drivers. Controlled alendronate data do not support disease modification or reliable pain improvement.
nodes.id: Intravenous bisphosphonate therapy for moderate to severe bone pain

evidence.reference_title: Fibrous Dysplasia/McCune-Albright Syndrome: Clinical and Translational Perspectives.
evidence.snippet: Intravenous bisphosphonates may be helpful for persistent, moderate to severe pain

evidence.reference_title: A randomized, double blind, placebo-controlled trial of alendronate treatment for fibrous dysplasia of bone.
evidence.snippet: no significant effect on serum osteocalcin, pain, or functional parameters.

nodes.description: Management is supportive and individualized, with treatment of endocrinopathies, pain evaluation, orthoses, physical therapy, radiologic surveillance, and subspecialty care based on lesion distribution.

evidence.reference_title: Fibrous Dysplasia of Bone and McCune-Albright Syndrome: A Bench to Bedside Review.
evidence.snippet: Management in FD is supportive, with the goal of optimizing function and minimizing morbidity.

evidence.reference_title: Fibrous dysplasia of bone (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0100013 | Neoplasm of the breast | Very rare (<4-1%)

nodes.description: FGF23-mediated renal phosphate wasting in fibrous dysplasia/McCune-Albright syndrome is managed with oral phosphate supplementation plus active vitamin D, such as calcitriol or another activated vitamin D analogue.
nodes.id: Oral phosphate and active vitamin D for hypophosphatemia

evidence.reference_title: Fibrous Dysplasia/McCune-Albright Syndrome: Clinical and Translational Perspectives.
evidence.snippet: Similar to other disorders of FGF23 excess, hypophosphatemia is managed with oral phosphorus and calcitriol.

evidence.reference_title: Fibrous Dysplasia of Bone and McCune-Albright Syndrome: A Bench to Bedside Review.
evidence.snippet: Hypophosphatemia is managed similarly to other diseases of FGF23 excess with oral phosphorous and activated vitamin D analogues

nodes.description: Post-zygotic activating GNAS variants cause constitutive Gs-alpha/cAMP signaling in a mosaic population of skeletal progenitors. Mosaicism is essential to viability and determines whether disease is monostotic, polyostotic, or syndromic with extraskeletal manifestations.
nodes.id: Somatic GNAS activation in mosaic skeletal progenitors

biological_processes: adenylate cyclase-activating G protein-coupled receptor signaling pathway

evidence.reference_title: Fibrous Dysplasia/McCune-Albright Syndrome: Clinical and Translational Perspectives.
evidence.snippet: post-zygotic GNAS mutations acquired early in embryogenesis lead to a somatic mosaic disease state

evidence.reference_title: Fibrous Dysplasia of Bone and McCune-Albright Syndrome: A Bench to Bedside Review.
evidence.snippet: somatic mosaicism, which allows for close association of mutation-bearing cells with normal cells, is essential

nodes.description: FD lesions weaken affected bone and produce pain, deformity, fractures, cortical thinning, bowing, coxa vara, gait disturbance, and axial or craniofacial skeletal morbidity according to lesion location.

evidence.reference_title: Fibrous Dysplasia of Bone and McCune-Albright Syndrome: A Bench to Bedside Review.
evidence.snippet: The resulting skeleton is weakened and prone to fractures and deformity, resulting in pain and functional impairment.

nodes.description: Surgery can preserve function, reduce pain, stabilize fractures, correct deformity, or address craniofacial functional compromise; prophylactic optic nerve decompression is avoided without visual compromise.
nodes.id: Surgery for fracture, deformity, or functional compromise

evidence.reference_title: Fibrous Dysplasia of Bone and McCune-Albright Syndrome: A Bench to Bedside Review.
evidence.snippet: Treatment often consists of surgery to preserve function and reduce pain
